skill.name: authorizing-api-requests
skill.description: Use when authenticating or authorizing requests to any Mailtrap API (Email Send, Email Testing/Sandbox, Templates, Contacts, Sending Domains, Suppressions). Use when picking the auth header, choosing token scope, storing tokens safely, or resolving the Mailtrap account_id. Use before writing or generating any Mailtrap API call.
skill.summary: Authoritative reference for Mailtrap API authentication including token scoping, auth headers, secure storage, and account ID resolution.
skill.author: mailtrap

security_audit.summary: All 75 static findings are false positives. The SKILL.md is a documentation file containing bash/curl examples in markdown code blocks, not executable scripts. The external_command detections target shell backtick syntax inside code fences showing example curl commands. Network findings reference legitimate mailtrap.io API endpoints. Environment access detections are from best-practices documentation on token storage. The skill is safe documentation content with no executable or malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Shell command examples in documentation code blocks
medium_findings.description: The static analyzer detected 56 instances of Ruby/shell backtick execution patterns. All occurrences are bash and curl command examples inside Markdown code fences. The skill is a documentation reference file (SKILL.md) that shows users how to use the Mailtrap API. No commands are executed by the skill itself.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: High confidence false positive. All backtick content is inside Markdown code fences showing curl examples. The file is .md format with no executable components.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded API endpoint URLs in documentation
medium_findings.description: The analyzer detected 12 URL references to mailtrap.io domains. All URLs point to legitimate Mailtrap API endpoints and documentation pages. The skill describes how to call these endpoints, it does not make network requests itself.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: High confidence false positive. All URLs reference official Mailtrap services (mailtrap.io, docs.mailtrap.io, send.api.mailtrap.io) which are legitimate API endpoints for the service being documented.

medium_findings.title: Environment file and dotenv reference in best practices
medium_findings.description: The analyzer flagged line 53 for env_access (dotenv library). This line is in a 'Where to put tokens' section that recommends using .env files for local development. It is educational content advising secure token storage practices.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: High confidence false positive. The text recommends using .env files with .gitignore and environment variables in CI, which is standard security advice, not credential harvesting.

medium_findings.title: Heuristic dangerous combination alert
medium_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged a CRITICAL heuristic combination of code execution, network, and credential access patterns. The SKILL.md file contains bash code examples with curl commands using env var tokens, which matches this pattern. However, these are documentation examples showing proper API usage, not active malicious behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: High confidence false positive. The three pattern types appear together because the documentation shows complete examples of authenticated API calls. There is no exfiltration endpoint, no automated execution, and no credential harvesting logic.

low_findings.title: Email sending capability reference
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged line 38 for 'Email sending capability'. This line references the Mailtrap API tokens documentation page. It is describing the Email Sending API scope in the context of token provisioning advice.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: High confidence false positive. The mention is part of token scope documentation with a link to official Mailtrap docs, not an actual email sending implementation.

low_findings.title: Secret manager product names in documentation
low_findings.description: Line 55 lists 1Password among recommended secret managers for production token storage. The static analyzer flagged this as credential access. The context is a best-practices recommendation for secure token storage, not credential theft.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: High confidence false positive. The text recommends using secret managers (AWS Secrets Manager, 1Password, etc.) to protect tokens, which is the opposite of malicious credential harvesting.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance pattern in curl example
low_findings.description: Line 116 was flagged for system reconnaissance. This is the beginning of a bash code block showing curl commands to the Mailtrap API. The skill explicitly instructs users to resolve account_id dynamically instead of hardcoding it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: High confidence false positive. The curl examples are legitimate API usage demonstrations for a skill about Mailtrap API authorization.

content.value_statement: Developers often hardcode Mailtrap API tokens or use incorrect auth headers, leading to security risks and broken integrations. This skill provides the authoritative reference for token scoping, auth header formats, secure storage, and account ID resolution across all Mailtrap API surfaces.
actual_capabilities: Generates correct Authorization headers in Bearer and Api-Token formats
Advises on minimum-scope token provisioning per Mailtrap API surface
Resolves account_id dynamically via the Mailtrap Accounts API
Recommends secure token storage per environment (local, CI, production)
Documents recommended environment variable naming conventions
Guides on separating sandbox tokens from live sending tokens
limitations: Does not provision or generate actual Mailtrap API tokens
Cannot detect which Mailtrap plan features are available for your account
Does not handle API rate limits or error retry logic
Cannot access or modify tokens stored in external secret managers

use_cases.title: First-time Mailtrap API integration
use_cases.description: A developer building a new Mailtrap API integration needs to set up authentication correctly. The skill guides them through token creation, auth header selection, and secure storage from the start.

use_cases.title: CI/CD pipeline token configuration
use_cases.description: A DevOps engineer configuring CI/CD pipelines needs to inject Mailtrap API tokens as environment variables. The skill provides guidance on using encrypted CI secret stores and recommended env var naming.

use_cases.title: API security audit and review
use_cases.description: A security reviewer needs to verify that Mailtrap API tokens are scoped correctly, stored securely, and not hardcoded in source. The skill documents best practices and anti-patterns for reference.

prompt_templates.title: Set up Mailtrap API authentication from scratch
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to authenticate requests to the Mailtrap Email Sending API. Generate example code in Python showing the proper Authorization header format, recommended environment variable names, and how to load the token securely.
prompt_templates.scenario: A beginner developer setting up their first Mailtrap API integration

prompt_templates.title: Select the correct token scope for a task
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to send a transactional email through Mailtrap. What token scope do I need, what API host should I use, and does this endpoint require an account_id in the URL path?
prompt_templates.scenario: A developer choosing the right API surface for a specific task

prompt_templates.title: Resolve account_id for account-scoped endpoints
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to call the Mailtrap Contacts API which requires an account_id in the URL path. Show me how to resolve the correct account_id dynamically using the Accounts API, and how to pass it in subsequent requests.
prompt_templates.scenario: A developer working with account-scoped Mailtrap endpoints

prompt_templates.title: Build a secure production integration
prompt_templates.prompt: I am building a production application that sends emails via Mailtrap. Show me the complete authentication setup including: token storage in a secret manager, auth header generation, account_id resolution, and separation of sandbox and live environments.
prompt_templates.scenario: A senior developer building a production-grade Mailtrap integration

output_examples.input: What auth header should I use for Mailtrap API calls?
output_examples.output: Mailtrap accepts two header formats. The Bearer format (Authorization: Bearer $MAILTRAP_API_TOKEN) is preferred for new code as it matches HTTP conventions and SDK-generated code. The Api-Token format (Api-Token: $MAILTRAP_API_TOKEN) is available for legacy clients. Use one or the other, never both.

output_examples.input: How do I store my Mailtrap API token safely?
output_examples.output: Token storage depends on your environment. For local development, use an environment variable or a .env file listed in .gitignore. For CI/CD, use the provider's encrypted secret store and inject as env vars. For production, use a dedicated secret manager. Never hardcode tokens in source, pass them as CLI flags, or paste them into chat or prompts.

best_practices: Use the Bearer auth header format for new code to match HTTP conventions and SDK-generated examples
Scope each API token to the minimum required surface (one stream, one project, one product area) to limit blast radius if leaked
Resolve account_id dynamically from the Accounts API each session instead of hardcoding it in scripts or config
anti_patterns: Hardcoding API tokens in source code, notebooks, or config files instead of loading from environment variables
Passing tokens as command-line flags, which leaks them into shell history, process listings, and CI logs
Using the same token for sandbox testing and live email sending, which creates unnecessary risk if the sandbox token is compromised

faq.question: What auth headers does Mailtrap accept?
faq.answer: Mailtrap accepts two formats: Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN (preferred for new code) and Api-Token: $TOKEN (legacy support). Use only one header per request.

faq.question: Where should I store my Mailtrap API token?
faq.answer: Use environment variables for local development, encrypted CI secret stores for pipelines, and dedicated secret managers for production. Never hardcode tokens in source code.

faq.question: What is account_id and when do I need it?
faq.answer: account_id is a numeric identifier for your Mailtrap account. It is required in the URL path for account-scoped endpoints like Contacts, Templates, and Sending Domains.

faq.question: How do I resolve account_id automatically?
faq.answer: Call GET https://mailtrap.io/api/accounts with your API token. The response is an array of accounts with id, name, and access_levels. Select the correct account and use its id in subsequent requests.

faq.question: Should I use the same token for sandbox and live sending?
faq.answer: No. Always use separate tokens with different scopes. A sandbox token should not be able to send real email. This limits damage if a token is leaked.

faq.question: What if my token has access to multiple accounts?
faq.answer: Filter the Accounts API response by name or access_levels to pick the right account. Access level 1000 is account owner, 100 is admin, 10 is viewer. Do not always take the first result.
